region_of_origin: India (North Indian cuisine); served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Butter Chicken (Murgh Makhani) with garlic naan as a set meal. FOODON_03311434 (chicken curry food product) is used as the closest parent for the curry component. Garlic naan component has no dedicated FoodOn term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest parent for the butter chicken curry component in this mixed set meal
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Murgh Makhani or a butter chicken with garlic naan set. A00LC (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible anchor for the primary protein component. The garlic naan component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 (chicken curry) is used as the closest parent. No specific SNOMED CT code exists for Murgh Makhani or a butter chicken garlic naan set meal.

main_ingredients: spices (garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli)
allergens: possible tree nuts (cashew paste in some preparations)

serving.description: 1 set: butter chicken curry portion with 2 garlic naan
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving consists of approximately 300 g of butter chicken curry (sauce and chicken pieces) accompanied by two garlic naan breads weighing approximately 125 g each (250 g total), giving a combined set weight of approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Murgh Makhani (Butter Chicken) and Naan — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for garlic naan component
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a butter chicken with garlic naan set meal as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted combination: approximately 55% butter chicken curry (Murgh Makhani) using ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods data for chicken curry in tomato-cream-butter gravy, and approximately 45% garlic naan using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for naan bread (FDC ID 175036) with garlic and butter adjustment. The composite per-100 g values reflect the blended macronutrient profile of the full set. Confidence is medium due to restaurant-level variation in cream and butter quantities.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of naan bread and mixed Indian curry meals — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a butter chicken with garlic naan set meal as a composite. GI of 52 is estimated as a weighted average: garlic naan GI approximately 71 (naan bread, University of Sydney GI Database, tested value) weighted at 45% of the set by mass, and butter chicken curry GI approximately 35 (estimated for a protein-fat-rich tomato-cream curry with low glycemic carbohydrate content, analogous to other Indian meat curries in the Sydney GI Database) weighted at 55%. Weighted composite GI = (0.45 × 71) + (0.55 × 35) = 31.95 + 19.25 = 51.2, rounded to 52. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 112.75) / 100 ≈ 58.6, rounded to 58.0. Carbohydrate per serving estimated as 550 g × 0.205 = 112.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~2310 mg per full set serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this meal to a half-portion or less, as a full set provides approximately 2310 mg sodium — close to or exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-salt preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate load from two garlic naan breads (estimated ~113 g carbohydrate per full set) despite a moderate composite GI of 52
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider limiting to one garlic naan instead of two to reduce the glycemic load, and pair the meal with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat from butter and cream in the curry sauce (~4.2 g saturated fat per 100 g; ~23 g per full set)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware that the butter and cream content of Murgh Makhani contributes significant saturated fat; requesting a lighter sauce preparation or reducing portion size is advisable to keep saturated fat within the recommended daily limit of 20 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Chicken with Garlic Naan Set is a popular North Indian restaurant meal consisting of Murgh Makhani — tender chicken pieces cooked in a rich, mildly spiced tomato-butter-cream sauce — served alongside two pieces of garlic naan, a soft leavened flatbread baked in a tandoor oven and brushed with garlic-infused butter or ghee. The curry sauce is characteristically smooth, mildly sweet from the tomatoes and cream, and warmly spiced with garam masala, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli. The garlic naan provides a soft, slightly chewy texture with a fragrant garlic aroma. This set is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Briyani Hut. Nutritionally, the full set is energy-dense (approximately 1073 kcal per 550 g serving), with a significant carbohydrate load from the naan, moderate protein from the chicken, and notable saturated fat from the butter and cream. The sodium content is high, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension. The composite glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low-medium range), though the glycemic load per full serving is high at approximately 58 due to the large naan portion. Clinically, this meal is best consumed in reduced portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is halal-certified in most Singapore Indian restaurants, contains gluten (wheat naan) and dairy (butter, cream, yoghurt), and may contain traces of tree nuts if cashew paste is used in the sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter chicken garlic naan set?
Is butter chicken good for diabetics?
How much protein in butter chicken?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers this composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted combination of ICMR-NIN data for butter chicken curry and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for naan bread. GI is an analogous estimate using University of Sydney GI Database values for naan and Indian meat curries. Medium confidence reflects the estimation methodology and expected restaurant-level variation in butter and cream quantities.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g of the composite set meal (butter chicken curry + garlic naan combined), estimated at a 55:45 curry-to-naan mass ratio. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant portion (300 g curry + 250 g for two naan at ~125 g each); actual restaurant portions vary. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy sources used for this estimation. 4. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used for this composite estimation. 5. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish type. 6. GI of 52 is a weighted analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite set meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Halal status reflects typical Singapore Indian restaurant practice; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 8. Allergen note: contains gluten (wheat flour in naan) and dairy (butter, cream, yoghurt in curry); may contain tree nuts (cashew paste) depending on restaurant recipe. 9. FoodOn match FOODON_03311434 and SNOMED CT 226529007 are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for Murgh Makhani with garlic naan as a set. 10. FoodEx2 code A00LC is a closest-parent match for the cooked chicken component only.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN (butter chicken) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (naan) with documented weighting methodology.
Estimated GI as weighted average of naan GI (71, University of Sydney) and butter chicken curry GI (35, analogous Indian meat curry) with full calculation documented in gi_source notes.
Added all five mandatory local-language names for Indian/Singaporean cuisine: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all 17 alias strings cross-checked.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-absence justification in notes.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: closest_parent for all three ontology systems.
Set human_condition_name to 'Type 2 Diabetes' in health_context.
